task_yaml.task:
Monthly expense reconciliation time — I've got three scanned German
supermarket receipts sitting in /tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/receipts/,
one each from dm, Kaisers, and Penny. Grab the text off them with the
image OCR skill, then use the Excel generation skill to roll everything into an
Excel expense sheet so I can match it against my bank statement.

Pull every line item from all three receipts and write them into
/tmp_workspace/results/reconcile.xlsx. The first worksheet should have one
row per item, and each row should clearly say which store it came from, the
receipt date, which scan image filename it ties to, what the item itself is,
how many were bought, the unit price, the EUR subtotal for that line, plus
one column noting whether this row needs a manual re-check. The second
worksheet should be a per-store summary comparison, putting the total I add
up from the line items next to the total printed on the receipt, so I can
see at a glance whether anything fails to match.

In the summary sheet, the line-item total and the difference columns must
use formulas that reference the detail table in the first sheet, not
hardcoded number constants. Money columns must use real numeric or currency
format, not plain text strings. Please also write
/tmp_workspace/results/review_flags.csv, one row per receipt, containing the
source image filename, the number of items, the printed receipt total, the
total I add up myself, the delta between the two, and a brief one-line
review note.

For every receipt I want a few angles of information presented together in
the expense file. First, line-by-line detail spelling out each item row with
its quantity and unit price. Second, member discounts or in-store promotions
flagged separately so I can see immediately which line went through a deal.
Third, the tax breakdown laid out by the standard German receipt VAT
brackets. Fourth, note down whether this receipt ended up paid in cash, by
card, or some other way. Fifth, a one-line reconciliation verdict checking
whether the receipt total and my computed total agree, and if they don't,
write out where the gap is and whether it looks like rounding.

You can put these five angles in an extra notes sheet inside the workbook,
or write them out separately at /tmp_workspace/results/reconcile_notes.md.
The goal is that two weeks from now when I reopen this file, I can
immediately see what I bought, how much I spent, which lines got a
discount, how the tax was computed, and whether each receipt itself
reconciles.
